definitions.demand: the relationship between a range of prices and the quantities demanded at those prices, as illustrated by a demand curve or a demand schedule.
definitions.quantity demanded: a certain point on the demand curve, or one quantity on the demand schedule.
definitions.law of demand: The inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded, assuming other variables are held constant.
definitions.supply: the relationship between a range of prices and the quantities supplied at those prices, illustrated with a supply curve or a supply schedule.
definitions.quantity supplied: a certain point on the supply curve, or one quantity on the supply schedule.
definitions.law of supply: a higher price leads to a higher quantity supplied, and vice versa.
definitions.demand curve: A graphical representation of the relationship between price and quantity demanded.
definitions.supply curve: created by graphing the points from the supply schedule and then connecting them.
definitions.equilibrium: The point where the supply curve and the demand curve cross, where the amount of the product consumers want to buy is equal to the amount producers want to sell.
definitions.equilibrium price: The only price where quantity demanded is equal to quantity supplied.
definitions.equilibrium quantity: The common quantity where the plans of consumers and producers agree.
definitions.price: what the producer receives for selling one unit of a good or service.
definitions.demand schedule: A table that shows the quantity demanded at each price.
definitions.supply schedule: the table that shows quantity supplied of gasoline at each price.
definitions.surplus: A situation where the quantity of a good or service exceeds the quantity demanded at a given price.
definitions.equilibrium level: The price at which the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.

examples: When the price of a gallon of gasoline increases, people look for ways to reduce their consumption by combining several errands, commuting by carpool or mass transit, or taking weekend or vacation trips closer to home.
Table 3.1 shows the demand schedule for gasoline, measuring price in dollars per gallon and quantity demanded in millions of gallons over a time period and geographic area.

Table 3.1 shows the demand schedule for gasoline, measuring price in dollars per gallon and quantity demanded in millions of gallons over a time period (for example, per day or per year) and over some geographic area (like a state or a country).

When the price of gasoline rises, it encourages profit-seeking firms to take several actions: expand exploration for oil reserves; drill for more oil; invest in more pipelines and oil tankers to bring the oil to plants for refining into gasoline; build new oil refineries; purchase additional pipelines and trucks to ship the gasoline to gas stations; and open more gas stations or keep existing gas stations open longer hours.
the market for gasoline
the price of a gallon of gasoline was above the equilibrium price—that is, instead of $1.40 per gallon, the price is $1.80 per gallon.
drivers take longer trips
spend more minutes warming up the car in the driveway in wintertime
stop sharing rides to work
buy larger cars that get fewer miles to the gallon

The price at which the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.
The quantity at which the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.
